text: Ah, Kristen Stewart, the one-time romantic leading lady, wannabe action star, feminist activist, and arguably the most successful mouth breather in Hollywood history. There was a time long ago, dear viewer, when her star was very much on the rise. She was landed in some of the biggest roles in the industry and well on her way to A-list status. Her name being uttered in the same breath as the likes of Scarlett Johansson and Jennifer Lawrence. Stuart truly had it

all. the acting talent, the charisma the beauty. You sure about that? You sure about that? Wait, really? Either way, by the early 2010s, she was definitely riding high in Hollywood circles. But fame can be a fickle mistress. Glory is fleeting, and success can depart just as quickly as it arrives. and a combination of personal drama, relationship controversies, box office flops, and public meltdowns eventually derailed her career, turning this aspiring A-lister into a bitter

irrelevant burnout, appearing mostly in rambling lawcow interviews in a desperate attempt to stir up controversy. So, let's take a look at another case of Crash and Burn, the Christian Stewart story. Stuart was born in Los Angeles in 1990 to parents who both worked in the industry. Her father was a stage manager and TV producer and her mother a script writer and supervisor. So obviously Stuart found herself drawn into the business from a young age. Her intention was to get into

script writing. But when a casting agent discovered her in a school play, she was soon booking small roles in feature films and indie flex. Her first high-profile role was 2002's Panic Room by David Fincher playing opposite Jodie Foster. Honestly, I'd completely forgotten she was even in that movie. And yeah, it's not one of Fincher's best works, but it's still worth a watch if you fancy a neat little claustrophobic thriller. By now, her profile was rising, and she was booking starring

roles in small but well- reggarded movies like the drama Speak and the indie movie Yellow Hankerchief. None of them really made any money, but they did establish her credentials as a dramatic actor. But it was 2008 Twilight that would change the course of Stuart's life and career forever. based on a cloyingly melodramatic YA series about a vacuous teenage girl with no real personality who falls in love with an equally vacuous but extremely hot vampire. You know, like basically every piece of YA

fiction written for teenage girls with main character syndrome. Somehow though the movie was a massive global hit grossing over $400 million worldwide and propelling Stuart and her co-star Shovelace, sorry, Robert Patson, to global superstar status. Almost overnight Kristen Stewart was the hot new commodity in Hollywood. And before you knew it, they were churning out Twilight movies so fast it would make Marvel jealous. It was doing for her what The Hunger Games would do for Jennifer

Lawrence a couple of years later, only with more teenage angst and mouth breathing. Seriously, what the [ __ ] going on with that girl? Does she suffer from lock jaw, or did she just never learn how to use her [ __ ] nostrils?

whatever. The Christian Stewart bandwagon was now rolling at full speed. She was exactly what the industry was looking for. An indie actress who could hold her own in big budget movies. Just interesting and unconventional enough to give the appearance of something new and different, but also just attractive enough to fit into the leading lady molds. Snow White and the Huntsman in 2012 proved that she could do more than Bella Swan, giving us a fresh new spin

on the fairy tale specifically designed for modern audiences where Stuart plays a princess who rescues herself. >> Yes., Yes. >> Remember, when, that, was, considered, a revolutionary idea? Anyway, the movie was a hit and went on to make almost 400 million against 170 million budget. Not exactly a stellar return on investment. And I'd argue that Chris Hemsworth and Charlie Theron did most of the heavy lifting in that movie, but it was still enough to prove her chops as a bankable

star. All great stuff, but problems were a foot behind the scenes. See, Stuart had been in a relationship with her Twilight co-star Robert Patson since 2009. It seemed like a fairy tale romance, two of the hottest young talents on screen with such amazing chemistry that they ended up getting together in real life. But then in 2012 it all unraveled in spectacular fashion. Stuart was caught cheating on Patson with Rupert Sanders, who just so happened to be the director of Snow

White, married and almost 20 years older than her. Oh, Kristen, you silly girl you. The revelations ignited a media firestorm, and inevitably most of the backlash was directed at Stuart. Overnight, she went from a hot young talent respected by her peers and beloved by Twilight audiences the world over to a virtual pariah hated by everyone. And if there's one thing Hollywood won't tolerate, it's PR liabilities. Big budget high-profile roles quickly dried up. And while Robert

Patson soon left Twilight behind for bigger and better things, Stuart retreated into low-budget dramas and mostly irrelevant indie movies for the rest of the decades. She eventually emerged from the acting wilderness for a poorly judged comeback attempt with 2019's Charlie's Angels, a movie that got absolutely savaged by critics and bombed at the box office. It did spawn one of the best memes about the absolute retardation of movie journalism today though. 2020's Underwater was an attempt to

slaughter into mid-budget action horror territory. Yeah, she wasn't really a box office draw anymore. But maybe there was still enough name recognition left to forge a second act career as mid-level talent, horror movies, low-budget action, maybe even a move into prestige television. No such luck, though. The movie got middling reviews at best and bombed at the box office. It was clear to everyone that the Christian Stewart party was well and truly over. Her box

office momentum was gone and her public goodwill exhausted. The years that followed were a slow, sad descent into bitterness and irrelevance. She ditched the Hollywood styling and the red carpet dresses in favor of a look I could best describe as meth addict chic. And most of her interviews consisted of increasingly vitriolic rants about feminism and oppression in Hollywoods. No, you see, she didn't nuke her own career with bad decisions and personal fuckups. It was the patriarchy keeping

her down. Really, >> the, thing, is,, you, can, only, get, away, with [ __ ] like that when you're operating from a position of strength and power. When your career is very much on the skids, and you haven't had a hit movie in over a decade, it just makes you look sad, bitter, and desperate for attention. A washed up one-time starlet who never quite reached the big leagues who was dealt a winning hand, but somehow managed to squander it, and is now too old, and toxic to make a serious
comeback. The world doesn't hate Christian Stewart. It just stopped caring about her. Anyway, that's all I've got for today. Go away now.
